content: Let's design the session-closer skill. We have three architectural decisions to lock in this session.

Sounds good. Let me lay out the three decisions explicitly so we have a clean record.

**Decision 1: use bin adapter**
What: Move every shell-out (gh / git) into a single `bin/session_closer.py` adapter; the SKILL.md layer never spawns subprocesses directly.
Why: keeps the LLM-driven layer side-effect-free and lets us unit-test the adapter as the single subprocess owner. Separation also makes the skill testable without a real gh CLI.
Alternatives considered: leave subprocess calls in SKILL.md examples (rejected — couples LLM logic to gh failure modes), or build a thin Python wrapper per gh command (rejected — explodes surface area).
Consequences: bin layer becomes the single audit point for `shell=True` / argv hygiene. Skill code never imports subprocess. Integration tests can stub the adapter with a Python callable.
Scope: issue (architecture-level but specific to this skill).

**Decision 2: decision marker protocol**
What: Each posted Decision uses an HTML-comment marker `<!-- claude-issueops:decision:<slug> -->` followed by `## Decision: <slug>` heading and four bold-prefixed sections (What / Why / Alternatives considered / Consequences).
Why: parsable by both gh (renders cleanly in issue UI) and the dedup checker (Tier 2 marker scan reads the comment prefix). The marker has to round-trip identically through writer and parser, otherwise dedup misfires on the next run.
Alternatives considered: JSON-in-comment (rejected — gh renders raw braces ugly), or embedding YAML frontmatter inside the comment body (rejected — Markdown renderers strip leading `---`).
Consequences: the marker prefix becomes a frozen contract — changing it breaks every existing record. We keep `marker_parser.DECISION_MARKER_PREFIX` as the single source of truth.
Scope: cross-issue (every issueops user repo has to honor this marker).
Good. What about the state file write story — we agreed it has to survive a crash mid-write.

Right.

**Decision 3: atomic state write**
What: Every per-session state-file write uses a same-directory tmp file (named `<file>.tmp.<pid>.<monotonic_ns>.<uuid8>`) that we fill, fsync, then `os.replace` onto the target. The read-modify-write critical section runs under POSIX flock so concurrent hooks cannot stomp each other.
Why: PreCompact / UserPromptSubmit / SessionEnd / session-closer all want to merge a patch into the same state file. Without atomic replace + lock we lose patches under concurrency, and without the parent-dir fsync we can lose the rename across a crash.
Alternatives considered: in-process lock only (rejected — different processes), SQLite (rejected — overkill for one tiny JSON file per session), append-only log + replay (rejected — readers would have to replay every read).
Consequences: a single helper (`path_utils.atomic_write_json` + `acquire_file_lock`) becomes the only place state-file mutation happens. Crash resilience is then a property of that helper, not every caller.
Scope: cross-issue (atomic writes are a cross-cutting concern reused by every hook in this plugin).
Locked in. Let's wrap up the session.
